cataloging_subject.label: Confessio Amantis
cataloging_subject.scope_note: John Gower's late-fourteenth-century English literary work in a prologue and eight books, including its embedded Latin verses, headings, and glosses and its authorial revision history. This record describes the intellectual work, not Macaulay's 1900-1901 critical edition, the EETS or Clarendon publication packages, Fairfax 3 as a physical codex, the OTA/U-M encodings, or the repository XML. The present digital representation transmits only the English numbered running text of Macaulay's Fairfax-based third-recension edition.

catalog_summary.title: Confessio Amantis
catalog_summary.author: John Gower
catalog_summary.editor: G. C. Macaulay (editor of the 1900-1901 source edition)
catalog_summary.date: Composed c. 1386-1390 and revised through c. 1392-1393
catalog_summary.region: England; the exact place of composition is not established in this record

notes: A functional English translation of the Latin title, not a separate medieval title witness.

titles.supplied_by: University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative
notes: Catalog/encoding title, not a claim that the possessive wording is authorial.

titles.value: Of hem that writen ous tofore
notes: First encoded English line, Prologue 1.
Final encoded English line, Book VIII 3172; the Fairfax surface also carries a Latin explicit and associated verse omitted from the repository XML.

agents.name: John Gower
notes: The XML gives 1325?-1408; current reference works commonly place his birth around 1330. The dating difference concerns the person, not the work's composition interval.

agents.name: G. C. Macaulay
notes: Editor of the 1900-1901 source edition; not a medieval coauthor. His edition chooses Fairfax 3 as running copytext while collating a broad manuscript tradition.

scope.description: Authorial rewriting and additions reflected in the conventional recension groups

notes: The three-recension terminology is a scholarly model of multiple textual states; it should not be read as three wholly separate works or a mechanically linear chronology.

scope.description: The 1900-1901 printed source edition represented by the digital artifact

notes: This is representation-level editorial responsibility, distinct from Gower's authorship and medieval revision.

scope.description: Initial composition through the first-recension completion

notes: The opening date is approximate; the first form is conventionally associated with 1390.

scope.description: Additions, rearrangement, and revised dedication/epilogue states conventionally grouped as later recensions

notes: The repository XML's flat 1390-1393 creation date corresponds more closely to the revision interval than to the entire period of composition.
Gower's Prologue says he writes a book 'for Engelondes sake'; this record does not convert that cultural association into a precise place-of-composition claim.

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Authorial Latin verses, headings, explicit material, and marginal gloss tradition accompanying the English poem

notes: The intellectual work and Macaulay edition contain Latin; the OTA/U-M/repository text selection omits it.

form_statements.description: A long narrative poem, predominantly in octosyllabic Middle English couplets. The form is cataloged as verse because all nine primary textual divisions are verse; embedded Latin prose glosses are integral paratext rather than alternating prose sections, so they do not make the work mixed in this schema.

genre_statements.label: Frame narrative
notes: Amans's confession to Genius supplies the frame for moral instruction and a large collection of tales.

genre_statements.label: Dream vision
notes: The work incorporates dream-vision conventions and visionary political imagery; the confessional dialogue and tale collection are equally central.

genre_statements.label: Romance

scope.description: Selected embedded tales, including Apollonius of Tyre

notes: Romance applies to embedded narratives, not uniformly to every section of the whole work.

subject_statements.label: Seven deadly sins
notes: Book VII's education-of-a-king material interrupts the otherwise dominant sequence of sins; Book VIII treats Lechery.

subject_statements.label: Classical, biblical, and medieval narrative traditions

scope.description: The large body of embedded moral tales throughout the eight books

summaries.value: Confessio Amantis frames a wide-ranging collection of classical, biblical, and medieval tales as the confession of Amans, an aging lover, to Genius, priest of Venus. Organized largely around the seven deadly sins as they bear on love, the dialogue repeatedly broadens into ethics, social criticism, political counsel, natural lore, and narrative exempla. The Prologue surveys division in church, polity, and society; Book VII turns at length to the education of a king; and Book VIII concludes the treatment of lechery and releases the lover from his service to Venus.

The work survives in multiple authorial and manuscript states conventionally called first, second, and third recensions. Macaulay's running text follows the third-recension Fairfax 3 manuscript while recording departures and other-recension material in apparatus; OTA 0063, OTA 1677, U-M, and the repository XML strip that apparatus and retain only the 33,444 numbered English lines of the Fairfax-based third-recension stream.

extent.description: The repository representation contains a Prologue and Books I-VIII in 33,444 numbered Middle English verse lines: 1,088 + 3,446 + 3,530 + 2,774 + 3,712 + 7,844 + 2,440 + 5,438 + 3,172. Its 339 DIV2 units are supplied electronic 100-line groups, not authorial stanzas. The count excludes Latin verses/glosses, first- and second-recension variant passages, apparatus, and print paratext omitted upstream.

content_parts.label: Prologue
content_parts.summary: Gower announces a book balancing pleasure and instruction, surveys division and corruption in church, polity, and the social estates, and uses the dream of Nebuchadnezzar's divided image to diagnose the fractured world.

content_parts.label: Book I: Pride
content_parts.summary: Amans encounters Venus and Genius and begins his confession. Genius examines forms of Pride as they operate in love and illustrates them with moral narratives.

content_parts.label: Book II: Envy
content_parts.summary: The confession turns to Envy and its branches, including sorrow at another's good, detraction, false semblance, and supplantation, with tales testing rivalry, speech, and loyalty.

content_parts.label: Book III: Wrath
content_parts.summary: Genius questions Amans about Wrath and its forms, using exempla of anger, contention, homicide, war, and patience to redirect destructive passion.

content_parts.label: Book IV: Sloth
content_parts.summary: The examination of Sloth treats delay, negligence, forgetfulness, idleness, and despair in love, countering them with stories of labor, fidelity, and purposeful action.

content_parts.label: Book V: Avarice
content_parts.summary: The longest book examines Avarice and related forms of possession and exchange through an exceptionally broad sequence of tales and digressions, including accounts of religions, idolatry, astronomy, and alchemy.

locators.value: vol. I ends at 5.1970; vol. II begins at 5.1971

content_parts.label: Book VI: Gluttony
content_parts.summary: Genius examines Gluttony, especially drunkenness and excess, and uses stories of appetite, intoxication, and self-command to test the lover's conduct.

content_parts.label: Book VII: The Education of a King
content_parts.summary: Departing from the direct sequence of sins, Genius recounts Aristotle's instruction of Alexander in the knowledge and virtues required for kingship, joining moral philosophy, political counsel, and advice for princes.

content_parts.label: Book VIII: Lechery and the Conclusion
content_parts.summary: The final book treats Lechery, especially incest, through major narratives including Apollonius of Tyre. Venus finally confronts Amans with his age, cures and releases him, and the poem closes in prayer for good governance and lasting joy.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME source, Confessio.xml.
evidence.summary: Supplies the U-M title and author statement, 1390-1393 creation date, Prologue and eight-book structure, English line text, NODE identifiers, line numbering, print page milestones, and explicit omission declarations.

evidence.citation: Structural inventory of General Corpus Confessio.xml, 14 July 2026.
evidence.summary: Counts nine DIV1 units, 339 supplied DIV2 groups, 33,444 L elements, and 934 PB milestones; verifies line totals P 1,088, I 3,446, II 3,530, III 2,774, IV 3,712, V 7,844, VI 2,440, VII 5,438, VIII 3,172; and confirms that the encoded content contains English verse rather than Macaulay's Latin and apparatus.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English, John Gower's Confessio amantis.
evidence.summary: Confirms the current public item title/author, U-M/HTI context, OTA U-1677-C relationship, and catalog description.

evidence.citation: John Gower, Confessio Amantis, ed. Russell A. Peck with Latin translations by Andrew Galloway, TEAMS Middle English Texts Series, digital edition catalog page.
evidence.summary: Identifies John Gower, Middle English and Latin, poetry, and fourteenth-century date; describes the lover's confession as a frame for tales organized by the deadly sins and moral instruction; and assigns relevant categories including complaint, dream vision, exemplum, romance, and advice for princes.

evidence.citation: Russell A. Peck, introduction to John Gower, Confessio Amantis, TEAMS Middle English Texts Series.
evidence.summary: Supports composition c. 1386-1390, revision in and after 1390 through the 1392/1393 state, the conventional three-recension account, Fairfax's third-recension place, and the work's confessional, exemplary, ethical, and political design.

evidence.citation: G. C. Macaulay, ed., The English Works of John Gower, EETS Extra Series 81-82 (1900-1901), complete cached scans.
evidence.locator: vol. I, Confessio pp. 1-456; vol. II, Confessio pp. 1-478
evidence.summary: Confirms source-edition title, editor, volume split at Book V 1970/1971, English and Latin content, line numbering, section sequence, incipit/last English line, and apparatus excluded by the digital representation.

evidence.citation: G. C. Macaulay, introduction to The Complete Works of John Gower, vol. II, The English Works (1901), Project Gutenberg transcription no. 71162.
evidence.summary: Explains composition and revision dates, the three-recension classification and its chronological caveats, Fairfax as running base manuscript, wider collation, editorial method, and line-numbering scheme.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, MS. Fairfax 3, manuscript 4864.
evidence.summary: Confirms Gower authorship, English origin, third-recension status, exact work boundary, section starts, scribal/revision layers, and Macaulay's use of Fairfax as copytext.

evidence.citation: University of Nottingham Manuscripts and Special Collections, 'John Gower, Confessio Amantis and other works (WLC/LM/8)'.
evidence.summary: The institutional manuscript description identifies Confessio Amantis as IMEV 2662 and cites Macaulay's EETS Extra Series 81-82 edition.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source-lineage manifest for Confessio Amantis.
evidence.summary: Provides the layered artifact -> U-M -> OTA 1677 -> OTA 0063 -> Macaulay EETS -> Fairfax 3 chain, parallel Clarendon publication, source-selection boundaries, facsimile mapping, rights evidence, and unresolved source-description conflict.

open_questions.question: Can a precise place of composition within England be established for the work or for particular revision stages?
next_steps: Consult current biographical and textual scholarship before assigning London, Southwark, or another locality as place of composition.
notes: England is secure as cultural origin; exact composition locality is intentionally not inferred from Gower's biography.

open_questions.question: Should future metadata model the first-, so-called second-, and third-recension states as related work versions with passage-level boundaries?
next_steps: Review current textual scholarship on recension terminology and manuscript groupings.
Map added, deleted, rearranged, and rewritten passages against witnesses before creating version records.
Keep the current record's whole-work description distinct from the repository artifact's narrower third-recension English selection.

notes: The source-edition publication dates are 1900-1901 and Fairfax 3's physical production date is late fourteenth to early fifteenth century; neither should be substituted for Gower's multi-stage composition/revision dates.

The repository XML header's 'Complete Works' volumes 3-4 statement is bibliographically wrong. Confessio occupies Complete Works volumes II-III, while OTA 0063 identifies the separately numbered EETS English Works volumes I-II as the immediate source.
Macaulay's edition is eclectic in apparatus and editorial intervention even though Fairfax 3 is its explicit running copytext.
The tags describe the whole work and scoped major components, not every embedded tale; romance is intentionally scoped to selected narratives.
IMEV 2662 is independently confirmed by the University of Nottingham's WLC/LM/8 manuscript description; the earlier draft's 2262 was a transcription error.

The verse form classification describes the primary literary divisions. Integral Latin prose glosses remain recorded as embedded language/paratext and do not create prose content sections in the present schema.
The complete public Fairfax 3 facsimile is linked through the lineage manifest and cached outside git; the metadata record uses folio locators but does not treat the image files as the work itself.
